video_title: The Equity Markets Are Insane Right Now | Jan van Eck
gist: VanEck CEO Jan Van Eck argues the historically extreme equity valuations (price-to-sales at an 80-year high) are justified by matching mega-cap tech profit growth (Nvidia +129%, Google +80%), lays out four specific headwinds threatening Anthropic (cost-conscious clients, Chinese open-source competition, trust/ethics attacks, corporate infrastructure control), and reaffirms VanEck's three 10-year themes — AI, the rise of India, and gold/Bitcoin as a hedge against US fiscal overspending — while flagging that Social Security's insolvency date has moved up to 2032.

insights.claim: US equity market price-to-sales ratios (data back to 1946) are more expensive by an order of magnitude than at any point in the last 60-80 years, a distortion Van Eck attributes almost entirely to technology company multiples.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Extreme P/S is a valuation red flag on its face, but Van Eck argues it's explained by margin structure rather than mania — investors should judge it against margins/earnings, not in isolation.
insights.quote: today's market um is more expensive by an order of magnitude than any other market, you know, over whatever for the last 60 years, 70 years. So, 80 years.

insights.claim: Mega-cap tech profit growth in the most recent quarter was extraordinary: Nvidia's profits rose 129% year-over-year, Google's 80%, and Meta's 62%.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: These growth rates set the bar for what markets are pricing in; a deceleration toward more normal 20-30% growth would be a meaningful negative surprise relative to current multiples.
insights.quote: Nvidia's profits going up 129%, right? Google's profits going up 80%, Meta 62.

insights.claim: Across the group of large tech companies Van Eck tracks, average implied forward earnings growth is running at 95% with a median of 40% — an unprecedented pace of profit expansion.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Investors should track realized growth against this 95%/median-40% bar; missing it is a much larger risk to current multiples than a normal growth miss would be.
insights.quote: the average earnings growth of these tech companies, these are tech companies, is 95% and the median is 40%.

insights.claim: AI and AI-adjacent companies now represent roughly 45% of total S&P 500 market capitalization, meaning the index's near-term direction is effectively tied to that cohort's continued earnings growth.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart, host, framing confirmed by Jan Van Eck
insights.so_what: A broad market downturn is unlikely while AI earnings momentum holds, but the index's concentration means any AI earnings deceleration would hit the whole market disproportionately.

insights.claim: Unlike the dot-com bubble, the top-10 US stocks' valuations have compressed back toward their historical average (about 20.8x forward earnings on average versus roughly 21.6x currently) because profit growth has caught up with price appreciation.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: This convergence of earnings and price is Van Eck's core justification for staying fully invested in mega-cap tech rather than trimming on valuation grounds alone.
insights.quote: the average is about 20.8 times forward earnings, and they're about 21.6 times.

insights.claim: Two years ago the top-10 US stocks traded at genuinely elevated multiples of roughly 24x to 34x forward earnings, which Van Eck says correctly warranted worry at the time; that valuation risk has since normalized as earnings caught up.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Shows Van Eck isn't a perma-bull on valuation — he flagged real stretch before, lending more credibility to his current 'valuations are fine' call.
insights.quote: well above 24 times forward, right? Up to up to 34 uh ish times.

insights.claim: The trailing 10-year S&P 500 total return through June was 15.5%, which is strong but not abnormal compared to four prior technology-driven bull markets, including the 1990s.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Provides a historical anchor pushing back on the idea that the current AI-driven return profile is unprecedented or necessarily bubble-like.
insights.quote: the trailing 10-year returns for the S&P through June is 15.5%.

insights.claim: Price-to-sales is a comparatively weak valuation metric for today's mega-cap tech because their net margins (around 50%) are far higher than the roughly 5% margins typical of dot-com-era companies, which is why Scott McNealy's famous dot-com-era critique of paying 10x sales doesn't translate directly to today's market.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Investors screening purely on P/S risk misreading high-margin AI-era tech giants as dot-com-style bubbles; margin trends and PE are the more reliable lens now.
insights.quote: instead of making 5% margins, they're making 50% margins.

themes.theme: Mapping AI 2.0 Winners, Vulnerable Players, and Moats

insights.claim: Van Eck segments AI-exposed companies into four categories: vertically-integrated players owning compute, models and customer relationships (Alphabet, Amazon, SpaceX); partial-stack hardware players (Intel, Cerebras); memory chipmakers (SK Hynix, Samsung); and software companies split between single-LLM vendors, AI middleware, and 'AI inside' incumbents like Microsoft and Apple.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: This is Van Eck's explicit tool for judging which AI-exposed companies will still have a durable moat in 10 years versus which get disrupted by the next wave.

insights.claim: Van Eck believes memory chipmakers SK Hynix and Samsung lack a durable moat because most of their current profit surge comes from price hikes rather than volume growth, leaving them vulnerable once supply catches up with AI-driven demand.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Flags memory stocks — up sharply over the past year — as a segment to be cautious on despite currently sitting at a critical AI supply-chain bottleneck.
insights.quote: Most of their profits, Adam, uh to your to your point, actually just come because they jacked up prices. They're not even increased volumes that much.

insights.claim: The South Korean stock market, driven by SK Hynix and Samsung, went essentially sideways for 17 years before exploding higher recently — a price pattern Van Eck calls distorting and extreme for markets.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A parabolic move after a 17-year flat base is a warning sign of a narrow, momentum-driven rally rather than a broad-based repricing of the market.
insights.quote: 17 years basically of no performance whatsoever, right? And then it explodes

insights.claim: Companies that embed AI without owning the underlying models or compute — Van Eck's 'AI inside' category, like Microsoft and Apple — are structurally capped at 10-20% earnings growth, both historically and looking forward, because they depend on other vendors for the core technology.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Investors expecting Microsoft or Apple to re-rate like pure AI infrastructure plays may be disappointed; they're better framed as a lower-growth, lower-risk way to hold AI exposure.
insights.quote: they're really relying on other people to provide that the AI models and compute, right? They're just kind of wrapping it.

insights.claim: Nvidia's compute demand is growing roughly five to six times per year while supply is not keeping pace, and Van Eck doesn't expect that imbalance to close soon — the core reason he says investors should stay fully invested in AI infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: As long as the demand/supply gap persists, pricing power and margins for compute suppliers should stay elevated; the moment supply finally catches up is the key inflection risk to watch.

insights.claim: Google has transformed its business over the past 18 months by answering queries directly through Gemini instead of serving 'blue link' search results, yet advertiser spend (including VanEck's own) hasn't declined because placement, analytics fees, and ecosystem stickiness still generate revenue even as click-throughs fall.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Explains why Alphabet's ad-adjacent revenue and 80% profit growth have held up despite a widely-feared bear case that AI answers would cannibalize search-click monetization.
insights.quote: we're not VanEck's not spending less money on Google, right? We still want to get placement through I'll call it the old the old-fashioned way.

insights.claim: A meaningful but sub-50% portion of Big Tech's reported profit growth (e.g., Google's 80%) comes from venture-investment gains — such as Google's stake in SpaceX and Nvidia's venture investments across the AI ecosystem — rather than purely organic AI-services revenue.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Investors modeling forward mega-cap tech earnings growth should separate organic AI monetization from one-off venture-investment gains to avoid over-extrapolating durability.

insights.claim: Van Eck frames Nvidia as a long-duration compounder akin to IBM — paying a dividend, buying back significant stock, and generating strong free cash flow — expecting it to still be rewarding shareholders in 10 years.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Reframes Nvidia from a cyclical semiconductor trade into a long-term core holding comparable to a dividend-compounding blue chip.

insights.claim: Van Eck lays out four specific risks to Anthropic: cost-conscious corporate clients cutting AI spend, intensifying model competition, public trust/ethics attacks from competitors and government, and large corporate clients moving to control their own AI infrastructure to protect data and costs.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A multi-pronged, specific bear case on one of the most hyped private AI names — relevant for anyone with exposure via Anthropic funding rounds or AI-infrastructure proxies.

insights.claim: Anthropic's annualized run-rate revenue was around 30 (billion) a quarter ago per VanEck's chart and Van Eck estimates it's now approaching 50, but he still expects a negative revenue surprise for Anthropic within the next 12 months given mounting competitive and trust headwinds.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A specific, time-bound prediction of an Anthropic revenue disappointment that investors in AI-exposed private or public vehicles should track over the next year.
insights.quote: I would I would be surprised if there weren't a revenue surprise to Anthropic over the next 12 months.

insights.claim: Chinese open-source AI models caught up to Anthropic's Claude in effectiveness within about three months, and since much of business AI usage isn't latency- or cutting-edge-dependent, cheaper alternative models are increasingly viable substitutes.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Commoditization of 'good enough' AI models threatens premium LLM vendors' pricing power; investors should separate genuine cutting-edge AI demand from commodity/bulk AI demand.
insights.quote: the Chinese models have caught up with Claude.

insights.claim: VanEck's own internal token usage on Anthropic's models doubled from about 10 million to 20 million tokens over the three months since Van Eck's prior quarterly appearance, while dollar spend has begun to level off, evidencing active cost-optimization and multi-model use rather than single-vendor loyalty.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A real-world case study of margin-compression risk for premium LLM vendors as corporate buyers multi-home across models to control costs.
insights.quote: our VanEck's use of Anthropic is is growing has doubled in 3 months if you want to be extreme. But what you can see here is that what we're spending um is leveling out a little bit.

insights.claim: Coinbase is a case study Van Eck cites where AI token usage keeps rising while AI spend falls, achieved by routing queries to cheaper models — including free or open-source options — rather than defaulting to a single premium model like Claude.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A likely template other enterprises will follow, structurally pressuring premium LLM providers' revenue-per-token even as overall AI usage keeps growing.

insights.claim: Palantir CEO Alex Karp publicly accused Anthropic, in a CNBC interview echoed on the All-In podcast, of learning how client businesses operate and then building competing products, citing Figma — whose stock fell about 73% — as a company allegedly harmed after cooperating with an AI vendor that later competed with it.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck, recounting Alex Karp's comments
insights.so_what: A live 'AI vendor eats client's business' case study — a due-diligence red flag for any company sharing proprietary workflows or data with foundation-model vendors.
insights.quote: Anthropic is stealing, you know, is learning how those businesses run. And in some cases, uh you know, competing with them and then competing with them.

themes.theme: Tactical Trades: BDCs, Alt Managers, and Semis

insights.claim: Van Eck reaffirms his roughly one-year tactical bullish calls from last quarter on BDCs, alternative asset managers (Blue Owl, Ares), and semiconductors including Nvidia, saying the Q1 selloff in BDCs and alt managers created a buying opportunity that has already started paying off.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A tactical, roughly one-year buy call on beaten-down BDCs and alternative-asset managers, distinct from Van Eck's longer 10-year theme picks.

insights.claim: Blue Owl currently yields about 9%, and Van Eck says he's satisfied holding it even if the stock price goes nowhere, because the dividend alone delivers an acceptable return, with fears around the common stock already priced in as of Q2.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A high-yield income thesis independent of price appreciation, relevant for income-focused investors weighing private-credit-adjacent equities.
insights.quote: Their their dividend yield is like 9% Adam. So, even if the stock is is goes nowhere, I'm I've made 9% over the course of a year.

insights.claim: Van Eck argues private-credit default concerns are overblown, noting high-yield debt defaults remain low and BDCs like Ares are roughly flat-to-up since Q2 despite elevated redemption activity.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Pushes back on a widely-discussed private-credit-blowup narrative — a useful counterpoint for investors worried about systemic private-credit risk.

insights.claim: Despite staying structurally bullish on AI, Van Eck now advises trimming (not eliminating) profits on outsized semiconductor and AI trade gains, framing this quarter's move as profit-taking within a maintained overweight.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A practical portfolio-management signal: rebalance out of outsized AI winners rather than chase further, while remaining structurally long the theme.
insights.quote: It's totally reasonable to be taking profits um, on some of your your extra gains in semiconductors or what have you, your AI trades.

themes.theme: VanEck's Three 10-Year Structural Trades: AI, India, Gold/Bitcoin

insights.claim: Van Eck frames AI, the rise of India, and hedging US government overspending via gold and Bitcoin as VanEck's three core 10-year portfolio themes, explicitly distinguishing them from shorter tactical trades like BDCs.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Gives investors a top-down structural allocation framework distinct from Van Eck's quarter-to-quarter tactical calls, useful for long-horizon portfolio construction.

insights.claim: Indian equities have gotten cheaper even as earnings continue to grow, which Van Eck views as an attractive entry point into a 'lighter tech,' pro-growth-policy market that he treats as a 10-year structural holding rather than a near-term trade.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A valuation-driven case to add India exposure now, explicitly framed as a decade-long hold rather than a tactical trade investors should expect quick payoff from.

insights.claim: VanEck has internal debate about Bitcoin entry timing but currently leans toward believing now, before October, is a reasonable entry point after Bitcoin fell from roughly the 70s to the 60s (thousands, USD) during Q2.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A specific, time-bound tactical entry signal on Bitcoin from a major asset manager, nested within a longer 10-year bullish structural thesis.
insights.quote: It went down from I think like 70-ish to 60-ish over Q2.

insights.claim: Gold's recent selloff was driven by Asian investors deleveraging after the shock of the Iran war and Strait of Hormuz closure, not by US inflation trends — gold hit all-time highs last year even as US inflation was moderating, reinforcing Van Eck's view that gold is primarily an Asian wealth story.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Reframes gold's price driver away from the conventional US-inflation-hedge narrative toward Asian wealth/demand dynamics — useful for forecasting gold moves around non-US macro shocks.
insights.quote: it's more of an Asian wealth story. So it's no surprise when Asia gets a gut punch from the uh Strait of Hormuz closure and the Iran war that gold sell sells off.

insights.claim: Van Eck is bullish on hard-asset and commodity exposure (citing copper as one example) as a necessary 'AI 2.0' complement to the AI software/compute trade, since building data centers and AI infrastructure requires physical materials and power, though not every related sub-trade has worked out.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Positions commodities and power infrastructure as a second-order, still-early way to play the AI capex boom beyond mega-cap tech and chips.

insights.claim: Van Eck cites a bipartisan policy shift favoring nuclear power as a beneficiary of AI-driven electricity demand, though some data-center and independent-power-producer stocks have paused after appreciating sharply.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Nuclear and power-infrastructure names are a volatile but structurally-supported way to play AI-driven electricity demand growth.

themes.theme: Macro Backdrop: Fed, Deficit, Labor, and Social Security

insights.claim: The Federal Reserve's policy stance remains a 'less interventionist Fed,' consistent with Treasury Secretary Bessent's Q4-last-year messaging, with Fed leadership's internal task forces expected to issue year-end conclusions that Van Eck predicts will surprise no one.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Signals no near-term monetary policy surprises; Van Eck expects any mildly contractionary balance-sheet moves to be delayed until after the midterms.
insights.quote: Their their conclusions will surprise absolutely no one.

insights.claim: Through May of the current fiscal year, the US federal budget deficit is running at 5.8% (down from a 6.5% peak), with tax receipts up 5% year-over-year and spending up 3%, but interest expense up 10% year-over-year and now larger than defense spending.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Rising interest costs outpacing revenue and spending growth is the specific mechanism behind Van Eck's long-term US fiscal-risk thesis and the rationale for his gold/Bitcoin hedge.
insights.quote: we are running at a 5.8% budget deficit. Receipts tax receipts are up 5% compared to last year. Spending is up 3%.

insights.claim: Despite widespread fears at the start of the year about AI-driven job losses, Van Eck sees no evidence of net job losses in labor market data — instead there is significant sectoral displacement, with healthcare and education (driven by an aging population) consistently the strongest job-creating sectors.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Counters an early-2026 bear narrative that AI would quickly show up as aggregate labor-market damage; the actual signal so far is sectoral rotation, not aggregate contraction.
insights.quote: there's a ton of displacement, but not overall job loss.

insights.claim: The Social Security trust fund is now projected to run out of money in 2032, a year earlier than the prior 2033 estimate.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated fiscal cliff investors should factor into long-horizon planning around entitlement reform, tax policy, and the durability of Van Eck's fiscal-overspending hedge thesis.
insights.quote: We're going to run out of money, Adam, not in 2033, but in 2032.

insights.claim: Absent legislative action, the projected 2032 Social Security shortfall would trigger an automatic cut of about 20% to promised benefit payments.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Quantifies the real benefit-cut risk facing retirees under current law, a concrete data point for retirement and fixed-income planning conversations.
insights.quote: In 2032, they will cut payments by about 20%.

insights.claim: A bipartisan proposal to eliminate the income cap on Social Security payroll contributions is circulating as one fix, alongside newly effective 'Trump accounts' that have the government contributing $1,000 per child born 2026-2028 toward a tax-advantaged investment account, though Van Eck notes the non-investing half of Americans are unlikely to proactively enroll.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck
insights.so_what: Both a policy signal (potential payroll-tax cap changes affecting higher earners) and a reminder that participation gaps limit how much new retail-investor demand these programs actually generate for equities.

themes.theme: Market Dispersion: The SaaS-vs-AI Quality Rotation

insights.claim: Stock-level dispersion within the market is unusually high, roughly a once-a-decade level, and the 'quality' investment factor has underperformed this year specifically because many quality-index constituents are legacy SaaS software companies now being disrupted by AI.
insights.speaker: Jan Van Eck, CEO of VanEck (citing colleague Angus)
insights.so_what: Standard factor-investing assumptions that 'quality' equals safety are breaking down where quality holdings are themselves AI disruption targets; investors need to underwrite each holding's AI vulnerability individually, not just its factor label.
insights.quote: AI is coming to eat everybody's lunch.
